notes.section_title: Introduction: What Are Oceans and Continents?

content.text: The Earth's surface is dominated by two major features: vast bodies of water called oceans and large landmasses called continents. When viewed from space, Earth appears predominantly blue due to its extensive water coverage. The planet has been affectionately called the 'Blue Planet' by astronauts because approximately three-fourths of its surface is covered with water.

content.title: Ocean
content.text: An ocean is the largest body of water on Earth. Oceans cover nearly three-fourths of the planet's surface and are continuous with smaller water bodies like seas, bays, and gulfs.

content.title: Continent
content.text: A continent is a large continuous expanse of land. Continents cover a little over one-fourth of the Earth's surface and are the largest landmasses on the planet.

Both oceans and continents play a vital role in shaping the Earth's climate. They affect all aspects of life, including plants and animals, and therefore impact human life profoundly. These features are interconnected and work together to sustain the biosphere.

notes.section_title: The Distribution of Water and Land on Earth

content.text: Oceans and continents are not distributed equally between the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. When viewed from above the North Pole and South Pole, the distribution of water and land appears different in each hemisphere.

content.title: Key Observation
content.text: The Northern Hemisphere has more land than the Southern Hemisphere. The Southern Hemisphere is predominantly water, with fewer continents visible. This unequal distribution significantly affects climate patterns and weather systems.

Oceans are not truly separate bodies of water with fixed boundaries. The ocean waters are interconnected, flowing continuously across different oceans. The water bodies are identified by conventional lines on maps, but in nature, seawater moves freely from one ocean to another, sustaining rich marine ecosystems.

The oceans together hold the most abundant water available on Earth. However, this seawater is salty and unfit for consumption by most land animals, including humans. Freshwater, which is drinkable and usable for agriculture, makes up only a small portion of the planet's water resources.

content.title: Freshwater Sources
content.text: Freshwater is found in glaciers, rivers, lakes, and in the atmosphere. It is also stored underground in the form of groundwater. Despite the abundance of water on Earth, the availability of freshwater is limited, which is why water scarcity and water conservation are important global concerns.
On world maps, five major oceans are identified: the Pacific Ocean, the Atlantic Ocean, the Indian Ocean, the Arctic Ocean, and the Southern (or Antarctic) Ocean.

content.title: The Five Oceans (in order of size)
items: Atlantic Ocean — the second largest ocean
Indian Ocean — the third largest ocean
Southern Ocean — the fourth largest ocean

content.title: Interconnected Oceans
content.text: Although we name five oceans, the ocean waters are interconnected. The divisions we see on maps are conventional lines; in reality, seawater flows continuously across all oceans, sustaining a rich and interconnected marine ecosystem.

The Indian Ocean is surrounded by important landmasses. To the north lies Asia, to the west is Africa, and to the east is Australia. The Southern Ocean forms the southern boundary of the Indian Ocean.

India is strategically located on either side of the Indian Ocean, which is divided into two important water bodies. The Arabian Sea lies to the west of India, while the Bay of Bengal lies to the east.

Oceans support an incredible diversity of life. The marine flora includes tiny plants called algae and various kinds of seaweeds. The marine fauna consists of thousands of species of fish, dolphins, whales, and countless mysterious deep-sea creatures.

content.title: Marine Flora and Fauna
content.text: Flora refers to the plant life of a region, while fauna refers to the animal life. Each part of the ocean, from the sun-lit surface to the dark depths, has its own diverse life forms adapted to their specific environment.
Marine biodiversity is crucial for the health of the planet. Ocean ecosystems produce oxygen, absorb carbon dioxide, regulate climate, and provide food and resources for millions of people worldwide.

Oceans have a profound impact on Earth's weather and climate. Large masses of clouds form over oceans and bring rain to the continents. The monsoon rains that fall in India during summer originate from the oceans. Without these rains, agriculture and all life on land would suffer greatly.

content.title: Ocean-Climate Connection
content.text: Oceans regulate the water cycle by evaporating water, which forms clouds and brings precipitation to land. This process sustains all life on Earth and makes oceans critical for maintaining global climate patterns.

While oceans bring beneficial rains, they also give rise to violent natural disasters. Cyclones are extreme weather events characterized by violent rainfall and very strong winds. These storms can cause widespread damage to coastal regions of the world, threatening lives and property.

content.title: Cyclone
content.text: A cyclone is a violent weather event originating over oceans, characterized by extreme rainfall and very strong winds. Cyclones can cause devastating damage to coastal communities and are among the most destructive natural disasters.

A tsunami is a huge and powerful wave that originates in the ocean. Tsunamis are generally caused by a strong earthquake or a volcanic eruption at the bottom of the ocean. These waves can travel thousands of kilometres across the ocean and submerge coastal areas, causing widespread destruction.

content.title: Tsunami
content.text: A tsunami is a large destructive wave in the ocean caused by underwater earthquakes or volcanic eruptions. Tsunamis can travel rapidly across ocean basins and cause catastrophic damage when they reach coastal regions.

content.title: Early Warning Systems
content.text: Tsunamis are rare but very destructive. Fortunately, they can often be detected before they hit a coast. Many countries collaborate in 'early warning systems' to protect people. The Indian Ocean Tsunami Warning System is one such initiative in which many countries, including India, participate.

Events that lead to loss of life and property are handled under disaster management. India has its own National Disaster Management Authority to deal with all kinds of disasters, including those caused by oceans.

Continents are visible on the world map, but counting them is not straightforward. The number of continents can vary depending on how they are defined. Different geographical and cultural perspectives lead to different counts.

content.title: Different Continental Counts
items: Four continents: Africa-Eurasia, America, Antarctica, Australia
Five continents: Africa, America, Antarctica, Australia, Eurasia
Six continents: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, South America (in some models)
Seven continents: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, South America

content.title: Standard Classification
content.text: The seven-continent model is the most widely adopted and used in practice. In this model, Asia and Europe are considered separate continents despite forming a connected landmass, due to their different historical, cultural, and geological evolution.

Some continents are further subdivided. North America and South America are generally considered as two separate continents, though together they form the American landmass. Similarly, Europe and Asia are treated as two continents due to their distinct historical and cultural development, though geologists sometimes refer to them together as 'Eurasia'.

content.title: Geographical vs. Geological Views
content.text: While geographical convention separates Europe and Asia as two continents, geological perspective sometimes combines them into a single landmass called Eurasia. This difference reflects how continents can be classified in various ways.

content.title: The Seven Continents
items: Africa — a continent south of Europe
Antarctica — the coldest continent, located at the South Pole
Asia — the largest continent, extending from the Arctic to the tropics
Europe — a continent known for its cultural diversity
North America — includes Canada, the United States, and Central American countries
South America — includes countries such as Brazil, Argentina, and Peru

content.text: The size of continents varies greatly. Asia is by far the largest, while Australia is the smallest. Relative sizes can be visualized on maps that show actual proportional areas rather than conventional map projections.

The five Olympic rings are one of the symbols of the Olympic Games. They symbolize the gathering of sportspeople from all over the world. The rings were chosen to represent five inhabited continents — Africa, America, Asia, Australia, and Europe. This representation highlights the global nature of the Olympic movement.

content.title: Island
content.text: An island is a piece of land that is surrounded by water on all sides. Islands are smaller than continents but larger than other land formations.

Continents do not include all landmass on Earth. Some smaller pieces of land are left out from continental classifications. Surrounded by water on all sides, these are called islands. Although continents are also surrounded by water, islands are not considered continents because of their smaller size.
There are lakhs of islands on the planet, of very different sizes. Some are tiny and uninhabited, while others are large enough to support significant populations.

Greenland is the largest island in the world. Its size is so vast that you would have to add the areas of the 10 largest states of India to reach its size. This demonstrates the immense scale of some island landmasses.

India has more than 1,300 small islands. Those include two major groups — the Andaman and Nicobar Islands in the Bay of Bengal and Lakshadweep Islands in the Arabian Sea. These island groups are important parts of India's geographical and political territory.

Since 1981, the Indian Antarctica Programme has been exploring Antarctica, a continent with a very cold and harsh environment. Antarctica is covered largely in ice and is one of the most extreme environments on Earth. In 1983, India established its first scientific base station there, called 'Dakshin Gangotri'. Two more bases were established later.

About 40 teams of Indian scientists have conducted research in this faraway region, especially on the evolution of climate and environment. The settlement where these scientists live includes modern facilities such as a library and even a post office, making it a remarkable achievement in extreme environment habitation.

notes.section_title: Oceans and Life: The Critical Role of Oceans

content.text: Oceans and continents are vital parts of the environment and affect most aspects of our lives, even if we do not always notice it. Oceans send rain to the continents through a process known as the water cycle, which is essential for all life. Without oceans, there would be no rainfall and the Earth would become a desert.

content.title: The Water Cycle
content.text: The water cycle is the continuous movement of water between the oceans, atmosphere, and land. Oceans evaporate water, which rises as vapor, condenses into clouds, and falls as precipitation on land. This cycle is essential for sustaining all life on Earth.

More than half of the world's oxygen is produced by the ocean's flora (algae and seaweeds). This is why oceans are often called 'the planet's lungs'. The oceans play a crucial role in regulating the climate and sustaining life on Earth by maintaining the oxygen-carbon dioxide balance essential for all organisms.

content.title: Ocean Flora and Oxygen Production
content.text: Microscopic plants (phytoplankton) and seaweeds in oceans perform photosynthesis, producing the majority of the Earth's oxygen. This makes oceans critical for the survival of all aerobic life forms, including humans.

Oceans have deeply impacted humanity in many ways throughout history. From early times, people have used oceans and seas to migrate to other regions, to trade in all kinds of goods, to conduct military campaigns, and as a source of food through fishing. Oceans have also nourished the cultures of coastal people all over the world.

Almost all cultures have developed tales and legends about the sea, including myths about sea gods and goddesses, sea monsters, and treasures from the sea. These stories reflect both the oceans' dangers and their blessings. The oceans have shaped human imagination, exploration, and civilization.

Scientific studies have shown how the oceans are polluted by human activity. Millions of tonnes of plastic waste are thrown into the oceans every year, choking marine life and damaging ecosystems. This pollution is a major threat to ocean health and biodiversity.

There are several other forms of pollution affecting the marine environment. Overfishing (excessive fishing) is another cause for the decline of marine life. The combination of pollution and overfishing threatens the sustainability of ocean ecosystems.

content.title: UN World Oceans Day
content.text: The United Nations has designated June 8 as World Oceans Day to remind us all of the major role the ocean plays in everyday life. The ocean serves as the lungs of the planet, a major source of food and medicine, and a critical part of the biosphere. Protecting oceans is a collective responsibility.

It is our collective responsibility to protect oceans for the future of the planet and of humanity. Conservation efforts, sustainable fishing practices, and reduction of pollution are essential to ensure that oceans continue to support life on Earth.

glossary.definition: The largest body of water on Earth, covering approximately three-fourths of the planet's surface.
A large continuous expanse of land, covering a little over one-fourth of the Earth's surface.
A piece of land surrounded by water on all sides, smaller in size than a continent.
Any large area of land, including continents, islands, and peninsulas.
Related to or found in the oceans and seas.
The plant life of a particular region or period of time.
The animal life of a particular region or period of time.
A large destructive wave in the ocean caused by underwater earthquakes or volcanic eruptions.
A violent weather event originating over oceans, characterized by extreme rainfall and very strong winds.
Water that is not salty and is suitable for drinking and irrigation; found in rivers, lakes, glaciers, and underground.
The salty water found in oceans and seas, unsuitable for drinking but rich in marine life.
Freshwater stored underground in soil and rock layers beneath the Earth's surface.
Seasonal winds that bring heavy rainfall to continents, originating from oceans.
The continuous movement of water between oceans, atmosphere, and land through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation.
A single large continent formed by the union of Europe and Asia, as considered by some geologists.
One half of the Earth, divided by the equator into Northern and Southern Hemispheres or by the Prime Meridian into Eastern and Western Hemispheres.
The variety of plant and animal life in a particular region or ecosystem.
A community of living organisms interacting with their physical environment.

summary_points: Earth is called the 'Blue Planet' because approximately three-fourths of its surface is covered with water in the form of oceans; the remaining one-fourth is land forming continents.

The five major oceans are the Pacific (largest), Atlantic, Indian, Southern, and Arctic oceans; although we name them separately, ocean waters are interconnected and flow continuously across all oceans.
Oceans and continents are not evenly distributed — the Northern Hemisphere has more land, while the Southern Hemisphere is predominantly water.
Most of Earth's water is salty seawater in oceans, while freshwater (found in glaciers, rivers, lakes, and groundwater) comprises only a small portion and is vital for human survival.
Continents are commonly counted as seven: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America, though different classification systems may count them differently.
The Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal are the two major water bodies on either side of India; India has more than 1,300 islands, including the Andaman and Nicobar Islands and Lakshadweep Islands.
Islands are smaller landmasses completely surrounded by water; Greenland is the largest island in the world.

Oceans significantly influence Earth's climate through the water cycle, bringing monsoon rains that sustain agriculture and life on continents; they also give rise to violent natural disasters like cyclones and tsunamis.
Oceans produce more than half of the world's oxygen through marine flora (algae and seaweeds), earning them the title 'the planet's lungs', and play a crucial role in regulating global climate.
Oceans have been central to human civilization, serving as routes for migration, trade, military campaigns, and sources of food; they have deeply influenced human culture, mythology, and traditions.

Ocean pollution (from plastic waste), overfishing, and other environmental degradation pose serious threats to marine ecosystems; protecting oceans is a collective responsibility for ensuring a sustainable future.
